confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Passages advanced to evidence-review. Score of 2 reflects the terminal-phase strength of the Soviet sacred political order in 1985-1991, not its historical peak. The order's founding claims had lost coherence by the collapse period. Confidence raised from 0.60. Capped pending chain expansion.

reviewNote: Score value 2/5 defensible: legitimacy-collapse mechanism (Gorbachev's resignation diagnosis, Alma-Ata dissolution) documents the terminal-phase sacred-political-order-strength — the order retains some institutional inertia but can no longer sustain its founding claims. Score of 2 (not 1) correctly distinguishes this from North Korea's frozen pathology. Confidence 0.65 reflects periodization ambiguity (scores the 1985-1991 collapse phase, not the historical peak). Ready for human-reviewed pending chain expansion to ≥4 passages — now resolved (Milestone 7).

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Score of 1 (near-minimum) reflects structural corrigibility failure: the order lacked early-warning mechanisms and when correction was attempted, institutional actors aborted it. Score above 0 acknowledges that Gorbachev's reform attempt itself constitutes a corrigibility signal — the system could produce a reformer — but the reformer's failure to prevent collapse and the coup's demonstration that the institutional base would reject correction support the near-minimum score. Lower confidence than other scores because the question of structural vs. contingent failure (could the reforms have succeeded?) remains contested in the historiography.

reviewNote: Score value 1/5 defensible: institutional-fragmentation mechanism (1987 CC Plenum failure, GKChP coup) documents corrigibility failure from both directions — correction attempted too late and correction aborted from within. Score of 1 (not 0) acknowledges Gorbachev's reform attempt as a partial corrigibility signal. Confidence 0.68 correctly reflects the contested historiography on structural vs. contingent failure. Ready for human-reviewed with the historiographic caveat noted.
